excerpts.text: The empire of China is the largest in the world. It contains a third of the human race. And not one of them has the Bible in his own language.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the London Missionary Society

excerpts.text: I now embark for China. Whether I shall ever return to my native land, God only knows. I go with no other aim than to glorify God by making known the gospel of Jesus Christ.
excerpts.source_title: Journal entry on departure from England

excerpts.text: The ship captain asked me: 'And so, Mr. Morrison, you really expect that you will make an impression on the idolatry of the great Chinese Empire?' I replied: 'No, sir, I expect God will.'
excerpts.source_title: Memoir of Robert Morrison (compiled by his wife Eliza)

excerpts.text: I have now been seven years in China, and today I have baptized my first convert, Cai Gao. God be praised for this one soul.
excerpts.source_title: Journal entry

excerpts.text: The Chinese language is the most difficult in the world. After two years of unceasing labor, I can barely make myself understood. But I press on.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the London Missionary Society

excerpts.text: I have completed the translation of the New Testament into Chinese. I pray that God will use it to open the hearts of this great nation.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the London Missionary Society

excerpts.text: I live in constant danger of discovery. The Chinese government forbids the teaching of their language to foreigners. My Chinese tutor keeps a vial of poison with him in case we are found out.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the London Missionary Society

excerpts.text: My dear Mary has died. She was my helpmate and my dearest friend. I am left alone in this vast empire, with no companion but God.
excerpts.source_title: Journal entry after Mary's death

excerpts.text: I have compiled a Chinese-English dictionary of some 40,000 characters. It has taken twelve years. I hope it will serve future missionaries who come to this land.
excerpts.source_title: Preface to A Dictionary of the Chinese Language

excerpts.text: I am not an eloquent man. I am not a learned theologian. But I know one thing: China must have the Word of God in her own tongue.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to friends in England

excerpts.text: At Canton I must work in secret. The East India Company tolerates me because I serve as their translator. But my true work is translation of the Scriptures.
excerpts.source_title: Journal entry

excerpts.text: Today the whole Bible in Chinese is complete. After twenty-five years of labor, the work is finished. Soli Deo Gloria.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the London Missionary Society

excerpts.text: I work sixteen hours a day — translating, studying the language, teaching, and serving the East India Company. But the translation of the Bible is my first duty.
excerpts.source_title: Journal entry

excerpts.text: I beg the Society to send more missionaries. One man alone cannot evangelise a nation of 300 million. I need help, and I need it soon.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the London Missionary Society

excerpts.text: I have founded a small school, the Anglo-Chinese College at Malacca, where Chinese youth may learn English and the gospel. This is the seed of future harvests.
excerpts.source_title: Report to the London Missionary Society

excerpts.text: When I arrived in Canton, there was not a single Protestant convert in all of China. There was not a single page of the Bible in Chinese. Now both exist. God has begun His work.
excerpts.source_title: Reflection on 20 years of ministry

excerpts.text: The loneliness of this work is at times unbearable. For years I was the only Protestant missionary in this empire. But God was always near.
excerpts.source_title: Journal entry

excerpts.text: I labor in obscurity. Few at home understand what it means to translate the Word of God into a language so different from our own. But the work must be done.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to friends in England

excerpts.text: Liang Fa, whom I trained, is now preaching the gospel with great boldness. He is the first ordained Chinese Protestant minister. The future of Christianity in China rests with men like him.
excerpts.source_title: Report to the London Missionary Society

excerpts.text: My health is broken. The climate of Canton and the unceasing labor have worn me down. But I would not trade one day of this work for a lifetime of ease in England.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to his family

excerpts.text: I have adopted as much of the Chinese manner of life as I can. I wear Chinese dress when among the people. A missionary must not seem a stranger.
excerpts.source_title: Journal entry

excerpts.text: The Chinese are a proud and ancient people. They regard all foreigners as barbarians. Patience and humility are the only keys that will unlock their hearts.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the London Missionary Society

excerpts.text: I brought my children up in Canton. They speak Chinese as their mother tongue. Perhaps they will carry on the work when I am gone.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to his sister

excerpts.text: After twenty-seven years, I lay down my work. I die in China. My body will rest in this soil that I have watered with my tears and my prayers.
excerpts.source_title: Final journal entries, recorded near death
